title: The Complexity of the Non-commutative Determinant
speaker: Srikanth Srinivasan
abstract: I will talk about the computational complexity of computing the noncommutative determinant. In contrast to the case of commutative algebras, we know of (virtually) no efficient algorithms to compute the determinant over non-commutative domains. Our results show that the determinant in noncommutative settings can be as hard as the permanent.

proof_stages.label: Noncommutative determinant problem and two complexity viewpoints
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the determinant of an n by n matrix over a noncommutative algebra using a fixed order of multiplication, then separates the problem into generic arithmetic-circuit complexity and algorithms for specific noncommutative algebras.

proof_stages.label: Algorithmic motivation from permanent approximation and matrix algebras
proof_stages.summary: Determinant-based randomized estimators for the 0-1 permanent suggest computing determinants over richer noncommutative domains, but the speaker states hardness results showing that exact determinant computation over matrix algebras would imply exact permanent computation.

proof_stages.label: Noncommutative arithmetic models and the target theorem
proof_stages.summary: Noncommutative circuits, formulas, and algebraic branching programs are defined and compared, known ABP lower bounds are reviewed, and the main circuit result is formulated: if the noncommutative determinant has small circuits, then so does the noncommutative permanent.

proof_stages.label: Adjacency-matrix encoding of ABP coefficients
proof_stages.summary: An ABP is encoded by a matrix of linear forms, decomposed into coefficient matrices, so that the coefficient of any monomial is recovered as a specified entry of the corresponding product of coefficient matrices.

proof_stages.label: Hadamard-product closure via matrix substitution
proof_stages.summary: By substituting the ABP coefficient matrices into a circuit for a polynomial f and taking the source-sink matrix entry, one obtains a circuit for the Hadamard product of f with the ABP-computed polynomial g.

proof_stages.label: Low-complexity filter for equal-sign determinant monomials
proof_stages.summary: Instead of using determinant itself as the second Hadamard factor, the proof constructs a simple ABP/formula filter selecting interleaved double-permutation monomials in a 2n by 2n determinant, all of which have the same sign.

proof_stages.label: Reduction from noncommutative permanent to determinant circuits
proof_stages.summary: After the filter leaves only the equal-sign degree-2n determinant monomials, substituting the even-position variables by 1 yields the n by n permanent, so a small determinant circuit would give a small permanent circuit.

proof_stages.label: Algorithmic hardness consequences for matrix-algebra determinants
proof_stages.summary: The same substitution/Hadamard mechanism implies that efficient determinant algorithms over sufficiently large matrix algebras would compute the permanent, and a constant-size version is related to Valiant's gadget proof of permanent hardness.

examples.name: Godsil--Gutman random-sign determinant estimator
examples.note: Randomly multiplying the entries of a 0-1 matrix by signs gives a determinant whose squared value has expectation equal to the permanent, illustrating why determinant computation can be used as an estimator for the permanent, although with exponential variance.
examples.engagement_reason: he sets up the random sign matrix, forms its determinant as a random variable, explains averaging it to estimate the permanent, and discusses the variance/repetition count

examples.name: Roots-of-unity and quaternion determinant estimators
examples.note: The complex-root and quaternion variants are presented as concrete later determinant-based estimators whose improving variances motivate asking what happens over still larger noncommutative algebras.
examples.engagement_reason: he summarizes the roots-of-unity and quaternion variants by stating their substitutions, norms, variances, and runtimes, but does not derive or analyze them in the talk

passes.reason: The roots-of-unity and quaternion estimators are systematic variants over larger unit/normed algebras improving variance, not classification exceptions or adversarial examples in the argument.

examples.name: Hadamard square of the determinant
examples.note: The identity that the Hadamard product of the noncommutative determinant with itself is the permanent shows the desired sign-cancellation mechanism and motivates replacing the unavailable determinant ABP by a simpler filtering polynomial.
examples.engagement_reason: he defines the Hadamard product and explicitly checks that the determinant Hadamard-squared gives the permanent by multiplying the ±1/0 coefficients termwise

passes.reason: The Hadamard square of the determinant is the permanent, a highly regular polynomial with uniform coefficients on permutation monomials, used constructively in the reduction rather than as a counterexample.
The Hadamard square of the determinant is the permanent, a highly symmetric and complexity-complete polynomial used constructively in the reduction.
The Hadamard square of the determinant is the permanent, with uniformly positive coefficients on the permutation monomials, and is used illustratively rather than adversarially.

The Hadamard square of the determinant is the permanent, a highly regular all-positive permutation polynomial and extremal hard/complete object in algebraic complexity, used constructively in the reduction.
